question: Explain the purpose of CMSIS in ARM Cortex-M development. What are its main components?
expectedPoints: CMSIS provides standardized software interface for Cortex-M processors
Includes device drivers and peripheral access libraries
Defines common naming conventions and functions
Contains startup code and interrupt definitions
Enables code portability across different Cortex-M vendors

question: Describe the vector table in ARM Cortex-M processors. What does it contain and where is it located?
expectedPoints: Vector table is at beginning of memory (address 0x00000000)
Contains initial Stack Pointer value at first location
Contains addresses of exception handlers (Reset, NMI, HardFault, etc.)
Contains IRQ handler addresses for external interrupts
Can be relocated using VTOR (Vector Table Offset Register)

question: Explain the difference between ARM and Thumb instruction sets. What is the advantage of Thumb-2?
expectedPoints: ARM instructions are 32-bit, Thumb are 16-bit
Thumb provides better code density (smaller code size)
Thumb-2 supports both 16-bit and 32-bit instructions
Thumb-2 combines high performance with good code density
Thumb mode allows mixing via BX and BLX instructions

question: What is the role of a bootloader in ARM embedded systems? List its main functions.
expectedPoints: Initializes critical hardware components (clock, RAM, etc.)
Sets up stack pointer and memory controller
Loads application code from storage to RAM/Flash
Performs basic integrity checks
Transfers control to main application entry point
Can support firmware updates (bootloader updates)

question: Explain the Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC) in ARM Cortex-M.
expectedPoints: NVIC is integrated interrupt controller in Cortex-M
Supports configurable priority levels for each interrupt
Supports nested interrupts (higher priority can interrupt lower)
Has up to 240 external interrupts with 256 priority levels
Provides vectored interrupt handling (direct jump to handler)
Enables easy interrupt enable/disable control

question: What is Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)? Why is it important in firmware development?
expectedPoints: HAL provides standardized API for peripheral access
Enables code portability across different microcontrollers
Simplifies application development by hiding hardware details
Provides consistent interface regardless of hardware changes
Reduces learning curve when switching between ARM devices
Common HAL functions: GPIO, UART, SPI, I2C, Timer APIs

question: Describe the function of linker script in ARM firmware development. What information does it specify?
expectedPoints: Defines memory regions (Flash, SRAM, external memory)
Specifies placement of code and data sections
Sets start and end addresses for each section
Defines stack and heap sizes and locations
Controls memory alignment and loading
Essential for proper firmware placement and execution
